trial_name: CURASMUR — Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for Out-of-Hospital RSI (2019)
citation: Guihard B, Chollet-Xémard C, Lakhnati P, et al. Effect of rocuronium vs succinylcholine on endotracheal intubation success rate among patients undergoing out-of-hospital rapid sequence intubation: a randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2019;322(23):2303-2312.
one_liner: In out-of-hospital RSI, rocuronium did not demonstrate noninferiority to succinylcholine for first-attempt intubation success (74.6% vs 79.4%).

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) requiring out-of-hospital emergency RSI and tracheal intubation, excluding cardiac arrest and those with contraindications to either paralytic or sugammadex.
pico.intervention: Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV for neuromuscular blockade.
pico.comparison: Succinylcholine 1 mg/kg IV for neuromuscular blockade.
pico.outcome: Successful first-attempt endotracheal intubation (noninferiority margin -7% absolute difference).

background.existing_knowledge: RSI aims to optimise first-pass tracheal intubation and minimise aspiration and physiological deterioration. Succinylcholine offers rapid onset and short duration but has contraindications (hyperkalaemia, malignant hyperthermia). High-dose rocuronium (≥1 mg/kg) can achieve a rapid onset comparable to succinylcholine in controlled settings, with a different contraindication profile, but produces longer paralysis.
background.knowledge_gap: Comparative evidence for neuromuscular blocking agent choice in out-of-hospital RSI was limited, largely extrapolated from peri-operative studies and heterogeneous emergency department/ICU data.
background.hypothesis: In adult out-of-hospital patients undergoing physician-led RSI, rocuronium (1.2 mg/kg) is noninferior to succinylcholine (1 mg/kg) for successful first-attempt endotracheal intubation (noninferiority margin -7% absolute difference).

methods.design: Multicentre, randomised, parallel-group, single-blind, noninferiority trial.
methods.setting: 17 physician-staffed out-of-hospital emergency medical units in France; enrolled January 9, 2014 to August 31, 2016.

inclusion: Adults (≥18 years) requiring out-of-hospital emergency RSI and tracheal intubation as assessed by the treating emergency physician.
exclusion: Any contraindication to rocuronium, succinylcholine, or sugammadex (e.g., malignant hyperthermia history, myopathy/neuromuscular disease, pseudocholinesterase deficiency, allergy, hyperkalaemia)

methods.intervention: Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV after hypnotic induction. Intubation recommended 60 seconds after injection. Direct laryngoscopy with metallic Macintosh blades (sizes 3 and 4), no stylet, Sellick manoeuvre recommended. Rescue: bougie, ILMA, cricothyrotomy; sugammadex permitted.
methods.comparison: Succinylcholine 1 mg/kg IV after hypnotic induction. Same laryngoscopy approach, timing recommendation, sedation/analgesia approach, and rescue algorithms (without rocuronium reversal).
methods.sample_size_power: Assuming 75% first-attempt success in the succinylcholine group and a noninferiority margin of 7% (absolute difference), 602 patients per group were required (power 80%, type I error 5%). Target enrolment was 1300 to account for protocol deviations.
methods.follow_up: Procedural outcomes and complications assessed during RSI and within 15 minutes after intubation; additional reporting during prehospital care until hospital arrival.

headline_bullets: Rocuronium did not meet the prespecified noninferiority criterion for first-pass success (absolute difference -4.8%; 1-sided 97.5% CI lower bound -9.0%, crossing the -7% margin).
Airway escalation (ILMA use, direct-laryngoscopy failure) was more frequent with rocuronium, despite a lower proportion of Cormack–Lehane grade II views.
Early complications were lower with rocuronium (18.2% vs 23.2%), including fewer severe arrhythmias and hypotension episodes, but these were secondary/exploratory outcomes.

outcomes_table.note: Among 15 failures: blind ILMA intubation (n=6), ILMA ventilation then hospital intubation (n=6), cricothyrotomy (n=1), face-mask ventilation to hospital (n=2)

outcomes_table.outcome: At least 1 intubation-related complication within 15 minutes

outcomes_table.note: Defined as ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation
Defined as a new episode of systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg
Exploratory post hoc definition (as per trial report)

safety: Severe arrhythmia within 15 minutes: 2.0% (rocuronium) vs 4.2% (succinylcholine), absolute difference -2.2% (95% CI -3.8% to -0.7%).
Hypotension episode within 15 minutes: 6.4% (rocuronium) vs 10.1% (succinylcholine), absolute difference -3.7% (95% CI -6.8% to -0.3%).
Cardiac arrest within 15 minutes: 3.6% (rocuronium) vs 2.1% (succinylcholine), absolute difference 1.5% (95% CI -0.1% to 3.3%).
Death during prehospital care: 1.0% (rocuronium) vs 0.5% (succinylcholine), absolute difference 0.5% (95% CI -0.4% to 1.3%).

domains.notes: Permuted block randomisation (block sizes 4, 6, and 8), stratified by centre. Numbered, sealed opaque envelopes used; emergency physicians blinded to envelope contents until allocation.

Single-blind design: operators were not blinded; first-pass success is clinically objective but operator-dependent. Time from paralytic injection to first laryngoscopy attempt was not reported, limiting assessment of whether onset time differed by group.

Randomised: 1248 patients (624 per group). Primary analysis (per protocol): 1226 patients (610 rocuronium; 616 succinylcholine). Did not receive allocated paralytic: 11/624 (rocuronium) vs 7/624 (succinylcholine); received the alternative paralytic: 1 in each group.

Paralytic dosing was protocolised with a recommended 60-second wait. Induction hypnotic use was similar (etomidate in ~88% of both groups; median dose 30 mg). Post-intubation sedation/analgesia and vasopressor use differed in post hoc analyses (e.g., norepinephrine 9.9% vs 16.5%).

Groups were broadly comparable (age mean 57.2 vs 54.6 years; median GCS 6 in both; mean SpO2 95.4% in both). Centre effects accounted for using a clustered model; prespecified subgroup analyses did not demonstrate heterogeneity by indication or patient position.

internal_validity.verdict: Internal validity is moderate: randomisation and follow-up were robust and the primary endpoint is clinically objective, but operator unblinding and unreported timing from paralytic administration to laryngoscopy introduce plausible performance bias; secondary safety outcomes are additionally sensitive to post-intubation co-interventions.

domains.notes: Represents adult out-of-hospital RSI candidates in a French physician-staffed system, across common indications (coma, self-poisoning, acute respiratory failure, trauma). Excludes cardiac arrest and patients with contraindications to either paralytic or sugammadex, limiting applicability to those higher-risk subgroups.

Most applicable to services using direct laryngoscopy (Macintosh blades) without routine stylet use and with access to bougie/ILMA/cricothyrotomy. Generalisation may be limited in paramedic-only systems, settings with routine video laryngoscopy, different sedation practices, or limited access to advanced rescue devices and reversal agents. Follow-up was confined to the prehospital phase; no in-hospital outcomes were assessed.

external_validity.verdict: External validity is moderate: findings are most generalisable to adult, non-arrest, physician-led prehospital RSI using similar technique and dosing, but apply less directly to other EMS configurations, modern video-laryngoscopy–dominant practice, and patients with paralytic contraindications.

strengths: Large, pragmatic, multicentre randomised trial in a high-risk out-of-hospital RSI context.
Clinically meaningful primary endpoint (first-pass success) with high procedural relevance.
Prespecified noninferiority framework with clustered modelling to account for centre effects and sensitivity analysis in the randomised groups.
Detailed reporting of rescue airway strategies and early complications within a defined time window.
limitations: Single-blind design with operator unblinding; procedural decisions may be influenced by knowledge of paralytic.
Time from neuromuscular blocker injection to first laryngoscopy attempt was not reported, limiting mechanistic inference about onset-related differences.
Primary per-protocol analysis is appropriate for noninferiority but may introduce bias if protocol deviations are related to prognosis.
Multiple secondary/exploratory comparisons without multiplicity correction.
Follow-up limited to the prehospital phase; no in-hospital outcomes, ventilator-free days, ICU/hospital length of stay, or mortality beyond prehospital care.
Exclusion of patients with contraindications to either agent restricts inference to settings where both drugs are considered acceptable options.

critical_appraisal.summary: A well-conducted pragmatic trial with a clinically relevant primary endpoint, but operator unblinding and unreported onset timing introduce performance bias, and secondary safety outcomes are confounded by post-intubation co-interventions, limiting confidence in the complication profile.
strengths: Large, multicentre, pragmatic design in a real-world prehospital setting.
Prespecified noninferiority framework with appropriate sensitivity analyses.
weaknesses: Operator unblinding creates potential for performance bias.
Unreported time from paralytic to laryngoscopy obscures onset-related differences.
Secondary safety outcomes are confounded by post-intubation sedation and vasopressor use.
No multiplicity correction for secondary/exploratory endpoints.

sections.authors_conclusion: Rocuronium did not demonstrate noninferiority to succinylcholine for first-attempt intubation success in out-of-hospital RSI, and was associated with more airway escalation but fewer early complications.

clinical_bottom_line.verdict: When both agents are clinically permissible, succinylcholine achieved higher first-attempt success than rocuronium in this prehospital RSI setting, and rocuronium could not be assumed equivalent under a prespecified noninferiority margin. Rocuronium use should be paired with careful attention to onset time and a robust rescue plan.
clinical_bottom_line.applies_to: Adult, non-arrest, physician-led prehospital RSI using direct laryngoscopy and similar dosing, where both drugs are acceptable options.
clinical_bottom_line.does_not_mean: Rocuronium is proven inferior or unsafe; the trial did not demonstrate noninferiority, which is a distinct statistical conclusion.
implementation_caveats: Ensure a 60-second wait time after rocuronium administration before first laryngoscopy attempt.
Have immediate access to advanced rescue airway adjuncts (bougie, ILMA) and consider availability of sugammadex for reversal.
The lower complication rate with rocuronium is a secondary finding and may be influenced by post-intubation sedation and vasopressor practices.

controversies.notes: Emphasised that the appropriate inference is 'noninferiority not demonstrated' rather than proof of inferiority, given the confidence interval crossed the noninferiority margin.

Differences in post-intubation sedation/analgesia and vasoactive support could influence hypotension and other physiological complications, complicating causal attribution of safety signals to the paralytic alone.
Clarified the intended interpretation of the noninferiority analysis and discussed the observed differences in sedative/analgesic use as potential contributors to haemodynamic findings.

Secondary analysis of CURASMUR data found first-attempt failure (23.2% overall) was associated with higher complication rates. A large emergency department airway registry study found no significant difference in first-pass success between succinylcholine and rocuronium (87.0% vs 87.5%), underscoring that observed effects may vary by setting, operator, technique, and case mix.

Recent ICU and emergency airway guidance generally supports either succinylcholine or rocuronium for RSI, with drug selection guided by contraindications, haemodynamic physiology, and system capability for rescue/reversal; CURASMUR is incorporated as part of the evidence base informing these recommendations.

unresolved_questions: Would the results differ with routine video laryngoscopy use?
What is the impact of strict adherence to a 60-second onset time on first-pass success with rocuronium?
How do these findings translate to paramedic-only or non-physician EMS systems?
What are the longer-term in-hospital outcomes (e.g., ventilator-free days, ICU length of stay, mortality) associated with paralytic choice?

numbers_to_remember: Noninferiority margin -7%; 1-sided 97.5% CI lower bound -9.0% (noninferiority not demonstrated)
quick_recall.one_line_takeaway: In out-of-hospital RSI, rocuronium did not meet noninferiority for first-pass success compared to succinylcholine and required more airway rescue, so if you use rocuronium, ensure a robust backup plan.
